perturbation.original_text: Distributor will comply with all country, federal, state and local laws, ordinances, codes, regulations, rules, policies and procedures, Including, without limitation, all anti-corruption laws, Including, the U.S. Foreign Corrupt Practices Act (Applicable Laws). Distributor can find more information about the Foreign Corrupt Practices Act at the following URL: http://www.usdoj.gov/criminal/fraud/docs/dojdocb.html, o r b y contacting publicsectorcompllance@cisco.com.
perturbation.changed_text: Distributor will endeavor to comply with all country, federal, state and local laws, ordinances, codes, regulations, rules, policies and procedures, Including, without limitation, all anti-corruption laws, Including, the U.S. Foreign Corrupt Practices Act (Applicable Laws). Distributor can find more information about the Foreign Corrupt Practices Act at the following URL: http://www.usdoj.gov/criminal/fraud/docs/dojdocb.html, o r b y contacting publicsectorcompllance@cisco.com.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will comply' to 'will endeavor to comply' weakens the obligation to comply with laws, including the FCPA. This creates ambiguity regarding the distributor's actual responsibility and commitment to legal compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: The FCPA requires strict adherence to anti-corruption standards. By changing 'will comply' to 'will endeavor to comply,' the contract suggests a less stringent obligation, potentially conflicting with the FCPA's requirements for absolute compliance.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Criminal Division | Foreign Corrupt Practices Act Unit Skip to main content MENU Foreign Corrupt Practices Act Unit Corporate Enforcement Policy Statutes Legislative History FCPA Guidelines FCPA Resource Guide Enforcement Actions A - M N - Z Chronological List Corporate Enforcement Actions Individual Enforcement Actions FCPA Opinions Opinion Procedure Regulations (PDF) Opinion Releases Index Summaries Opinion Procedure Releases Review Procedure Releases International Agreements Anti-Corruption Sites Foreign Corrupt Practices Act Unit Share Facebook X LinkedIn Email An Overview Foreign Corrupt Practices Act The Foreign Corrupt Practices Act of 1977, as amended, 15 U.S.C. §§ 78dd-1, et seq. ("FCPA"), was enacted for the purpose of making it unlawful for certain classes of persons and entities to make payments to foreign government officials to assist in obtaining or retaining business. Specifically, the anti-bribery provisions of the FCPA prohibit the willful use of the mails or any means of instrumentality of interstate commerce corruptly in furtherance of any offer, payment, promise to pay, or authorization of the payment of money or anything of value to any person, while knowing that all or a portion of such money or thing of value will be offered, given or promised, directly or indirectly, to a foreign official to influence the foreign official in his or her official capacity, induce the foreign official to do or omit to do an act in violation of his or her lawful duty, or to secure any improper advantage in order to assist in obtaining or retaining business for or with, or directing business to, any person. Since 1977, the anti-bribery provisions of the FCPA have applied to all U.S. persons and certain foreign issuers of securities. With the enactment of certain amendments in 1998, the anti-bribery provisions of the FCPA now also apply to foreign firms and persons who cause, directly or through agents, an act in furtherance of such a corrupt payment to take place within the territory of the United States. The FCPA also requires companies whose securities are listed in the United States to meet its accounting provisions. See 15 U.S.C. § 78m. These accounting provisions, which were designed to operate in tandem with the anti-bribery provisions of the FCPA, require corporations covered by the provisions to (a) make and keep books and records that accurately and fairly reflect the transactions of the corporation and (b) devise and maintain an adequate system of internal accounting controls. perturbation.justification: The original text states "Distributor will comply," indicating a firm obligation. The changed text weakens this obligation by stating "Distributor will endeavor to comply." This introduces ambiguity and suggests that compliance is not guaranteed, which contradicts the strict adherence expected under the FCPA, as supported by the Law Explanation. Snippet 1 states that the FCPA was enacted for the purpose of making it unlawful for certain classes of persons and entities to make payments to foreign government officials to assist in obtaining or retaining business, which implies a strict standard. Changing 'will comply' to 'will endeavor to comply' suggests a less stringent obligation, potentially conflicting with the FCPA's requirements.

perturbation.original_text: Distributor shall not take any action or permit or authorize any action in violation of the Applicable Laws
perturbation.changed_text: Distributor shall not knowingly take any action or knowingly permit or authorize any action in violation of the Applicable Laws
perturbation.explanation: Adding the word 'knowingly' adds a requirement of mens rea (guilty mind) that may not be required under all applicable laws, including strict liability offenses. This shift introduces a potential loophole, suggesting that unintentional violations are acceptable, which contradicts the principle of strict liability for some regulatory offenses.
perturbation.law_explanation: RCRA imposes strict liability for certain violations related to hazardous waste management. By adding 'knowingly,' the contract implies that a lack of awareness could excuse a violation, which directly contradicts the strict liability provisions of RCRA.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Resource Conservation and Recovery Act (RCRA) Overview | US EPA Skip to main content Official websites use .gov A .gov website belongs to an official government organization in the United States. Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock A locked padlock ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. JavaScript appears to be disabled on this computer. Please click here to see any active alerts . Resource Conservation and Recovery Act (RCRA) Overview Access the Statute and Amendments The United States Code (U.S.C.) is the codification by subject matter of the general and permanent laws of the United States. Access the federal solid waste statutes and amendments (42 U.S.C. §6901 et. seq.) from the United States House of Representatives. To access subsequent versions of the U.S.C., visit the Government Publishing Office web page . RCRA gives EPA the authority to control hazardous waste from the "cradle-to-grave." This includes the generation, transportation, treatment, storage and disposal of hazardous waste. To achieve this, EPA develops regulations, guidance and policies that ensure the safe management and cleanup of solid and hazardous waste, and programs that encourage source reduction and beneficial reuse. On this page: What is RCRA? How Does RCRA Work? How We Safely Manage Hazardous Waste in the United States RCRA Today What is RCRA? What we commonly know as RCRA is actually a combination of the first federal solid waste statutes and all subsequent amendments. Learn more on our History of RCRA web page . These statutes and amendments describe the waste management program mandated by Congress that gave EPA authority to develop the RCRA program. Additionally, the term RCRA is often used interchangeably to refer to the statutes and amendments, the regulations and EPA policy and guidance. The difference is that EPA regulations carry out the congressional intent by providing explicit, legally enforceable requirements for waste management. These regulations can be found in title 40 of the Code of Federal Regulations (CFR), parts 239 through 282 . EPA guidance documents and policy directives clarify issues related to the implementation of the regulations. Check out the RCRA Tools and Resources webpage to find RCRA guidance and policy directives. How Does RCRA Work? Opportunities for Public Participation The public plays a key role in RCRA by providing input and comments during almost every stage of the program’s development and implementation through rulemaking participation and comments on treatment, storage and disposal facility permits. Find more information on public participation . RCRA establishes the framework for a natio

perturbation.justification: The addition of "knowingly" creates a legal contradiction under the misaligned terminology definition. The original text requires the distributor to avoid actions violating 'Applicable Laws,' whereas the modified text adds a requirement of mens rea (guilty mind), suggesting unintentional violations are acceptable. This contradicts strict liability offenses under RCRA, where liability can exist without intent. Snippet 1 indicates that RCRA gives the EPA authority to control hazardous waste from "cradle-to-grave" and that EPA regulations provide explicit, legally enforceable requirements for waste management. Thus, adding a 'knowingly' requirement undermines the strict liability aspect that could be imposed under RCRA.

perturbation.original_text: Distributor's key personnel who directly support Cisco's account have or will have completed training (provided by Distributor, Cisco Cisco's on-line anti-corruption training is available in numerous languages and is free of charge for up to five of Distributor's personnel at http://corpedia.com/clients/cisco/pre_reg.asp?lid-300446001., or another third party) on compliance with applicable anti-corruption laws within the past 12 months (from the date when this Agreement becomes effective)
perturbation.changed_text: Distributor's key personnel who directly support Cisco's account may complete training (provided by Distributor, Cisco Cisco's on-line anti-corruption training is available in numerous languages and is free of charge for up to five of Distributor's personnel at http://corpedia.com/clients/cisco/pre_reg.asp?lid-300446001., or another third party) on compliance with applicable anti-corruption laws within a reasonable timeframe
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'have or will have completed training' to 'may complete training' weakens the mandatory requirement for training. Also, changing 'within the past 12 months' to 'within a reasonable timeframe' makes the requirement vague. This potentially contradicts the FCPA's emphasis on proactive compliance measures.
perturbation.law_explanation: Although the FCPA does not explicitly mandate training, effective compliance programs require ongoing and regular training, and the change removes a proactive component of such compliance. This weakens the contractual assurance of proactive anti-corruption measures.

perturbation.justification: The change from 'have or will have completed training' to 'may complete training' weakens the mandatory requirement for anti-corruption compliance training. The original text ensures proactive compliance with applicable anti-corruption laws, while the modified text makes the training optional. The FCPA emphasizes proactive measures to prevent corruption. Although Snippet 1 mentions the FCPA's anti-bribery provisions and Snippet 2 offers general information, the core contradiction lies in weakening a proactive compliance measure, as highlighted in the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation.
